Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I tried to set aside time on weekends to catch up, but with 14 online courses it felt overwhelming. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to do practice problems or work through things physically rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd study a lot, other weeks barely anything because I just couldn't find the motivation.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. I didn't have a set schedule because life kept getting in the way. When deadlines were coming up, I'd cram and try to get things done, but otherwise I'd let things slide. It wasn't consistent at all, which probably hurt me in the end.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification about something being due or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes my peer support group would message me and remind me to look at something specific. I rarely logged in just to browse or explore on my own unless I was feeling particularly motivated that day.
Q4: There was a brief period where I tried to make it a habit to log in every morning with my coffee. That lasted maybe two weeks before other things took over. The peer support program helped a bit because my study buddy would check in, and that made me feel like I should at least log in to not let them down.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful for me because I could pause and rewind when I didn't understand something. I also liked the discussion boards because talking things through with other students helped me understand concepts better than just reading. When I could actually do something interactive, that stuck with me more than passive reading.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was exhausting. I also ignored a lot of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could actually practice something hands-on or work through a problem, that felt valuable. But if it was just reading theory without application, I'd lose interest quickly because it didn't feel like it was helping me learn.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. Required ones at least had a clear purpose and deadline, so I knew I had to do them. Optional stuff always got pushed to the bottom of my list, and honestly, I rarely got to them because I was just trying to survive the required work.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I had good wifi and watch them later on the bus or at home. I borrowed textbooks from the library and worked through problems by hand in a notebook. A lot of my actual learning happened with pen and paper, not on the computer.
Q10: There were many times when I'd meet up with my peer support buddy and we'd work through problems together on paper for hours. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also made flashcards and studied them while waiting for things or during breaks at my part-time job. That kind of studying was invisible online.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd watch a video once online, download the slides or notes, and then study from those printed materials for days afterward. I'd also screenshot important diagrams on my phone and review them repeatedly. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with those materials later.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was juggling too many courses and my attendance dropped because I had to prioritize work shifts to afford basic things. The anxiety started building up even though my stress was generally manageable. I felt like I was falling behind and couldn't catch up no matter what I did.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. By evening, after work or a long day, I just couldn't focus on the LMS properly. Competing responsibilities like helping at home and working part-time meant the course often came last. When I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else. I'd technically be 'watching' but not actually learning anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed a lot of assignments because they felt too big to tackle when I was already tired or anxious. Even when I knew something was important, I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy. It was a bad cycle because putting things off just made the anxiety worse, but I couldn't seem to break it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to finish. Without a deadline, I'd let things drift indefinitely. The LMS probably shows all my activity clustered around due dates rather than spread out evenly.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But sometimes feedback came too late to be useful, or it was so brief I didn't know how to improve. My peer support buddy giving me informal feedback actually helped more than the official comments sometimes.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with so many modules and courses running at once. I had trouble keeping track of what was due when across 14 different courses. A clearer, simpler structure with fewer competing deadlines would have helped me stay on top of things better.
Q19: The fact that videos could be downloaded made engagement easier since I have limited internet access sometimes. But the sheer volume of content made it harder to know what to prioritize. Having more hands-on activities would have suited my learning style better than all the reading-heavy materials.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying but not understanding what they need to do, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing work. You might log in a lot looking for help or trying to figure things out, but if you're confused or overwhelmed, that doesn't translate into submitted assignments.
Q21: That actually describes some of my better weeks. When I studied offline with downloaded materials or with my peer buddy, I wasn't showing much LMS activity but I was actually learning. Some people just work better away from screens, and their understanding doesn't show up in login counts or video minutes.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually understanding or just going through motions. They also completely miss offline studying, peer discussions, and the mental effort you put in away from the computer.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things, but genuinely trying to grasp concepts and apply them. For me, engagement feels like being curious and wanting to figure things out, not just checking boxes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support buddy helped keep me going emotionally, or the hours I spent studying with physical materials away from screens. It wouldn't capture my genuine interest in certain topics even when I couldn't submit work on time, or how hard I tried despite limited resources and competing responsibilities.
